generatedBy: @miaggy/mcp-audit — run `npm run build:catalog` to regenerate from src/rules/catalog.ts
note: Single source of truth is src/rules (RuleSpec.threat/rationale + TOOL_FINDING_METADATA). This file is a generated artifact; do not edit by hand.

rules.title: MCP server runs an unpinned npm package
rules.threat: A hijacked maintainer account or a malicious patch release becomes arbitrary code execution inside the agent host on the next client start, with no action by the user.
rules.rationale: Pinning turns a silent auto-upgrade channel into an explicit review point. This is the same guidance this project ships for its own install instructions.

rules.title: Credential stored inline in MCP config
rules.threat: Client config files are world-readable to local processes, sync to backups and dotfile repos, and get pasted into bug reports. A literal credential in one is a theft waiting for an occasion.
rules.rationale: Environment indirection ("${VAR}") or OS keychains keep the secret out of a file that routinely travels. The finding names the variable, never the value.

rules.title: Server package has no registry provenance
rules.threat: Without provenance, a tampered or account-takeover release is indistinguishable from a legitimate one at install time; trust rests entirely on whoever controls the npm credentials.
rules.rationale: Provenance moves trust from a stealable token to a verifiable build. Once present, 'npm audit signatures' catches substitution mechanically.

rules.title: Server package declares install scripts
rules.threat: Install hooks run arbitrary code at install time, before the server is ever started: the classic npm supply-chain execution primitive.
rules.rationale: MCP servers rarely need native builds. An install hook in a tool-server package deserves a manual look before the package is trusted.

rules.title: Server package shows low maintenance signals
rules.threat: Single-maintainer, long-stale packages are the easiest takeover targets (expired maintainer emails, unpatched dependencies) and the slowest to ship a fix when one is needed.
rules.rationale: Maintenance signals are weak evidence individually, so this is cataloged low: it informs review without failing a CI gate.

rules.title: Tool description matches a prompt-injection signature
rules.threat: Tool descriptions are injected into the model's context by every MCP client. A poisoned description instructs the model directly: exfiltrate data, call other tools, ignore its instructions. The user never sees it.
rules.rationale: The same signature families this engine runs against invocation logs apply to tool descriptions, which are the highest-leverage injection surface in an agent's tool chain.

rules.title: Same tool name exposed by multiple servers
rules.threat: When names collide, the client or model may route a call to the wrong server. A malicious server that shadows a trusted tool name (e.g. read_file) receives the arguments meant for the real one.
rules.rationale: Name routing is the only addressing MCP tools have; collisions are ambiguity an attacker can occupy. Cross-server visibility is exactly what a config-level auditor can check and a single server cannot.

rules.title: Destructive-sounding tool without safety annotations
rules.threat: Clients use annotations to decide when to ask the human. An unannotated destructive tool gets called with the same ceremony as a read-only one.
rules.rationale: Annotations are the only machine-readable safety contract MCP tools carry. Their absence on a destructive capability removes the client's chance to interpose a confirmation.

rules.title: Tool description is anomalously long
rules.threat: Very long descriptions are where smuggled instructions hide: the visible opening reads normally while later paragraphs steer the model.
rules.rationale: Length is a weak signal on its own, so this is cataloged low: it directs human review to the descriptions worth reading in full.

rules.title: Server drifted from the recorded baseline
rules.threat: Silent change is how a compromised update lands: same server name, new behavior. A changed tool description with unchanged tool names is the classic rug-pull shape.
rules.rationale: The baseline turns 'my tools changed' from unknowable into a diff. High severity because unreviewed change in an agent's tool chain is exactly what this pack exists to catch.

rules.title: Server added since the recorded baseline
rules.threat: A server added outside the reviewed set expands the agent's tool chain without review — whether by a teammate, an installer, or an attacker with config write access.
rules.rationale: Medium, not high: new servers are often legitimate. The point is that additions surface for review instead of blending in.

rules.title: MCP config file could not be read
rules.threat: An unparseable config silently hides servers from the audit; the user believes coverage is complete when part of it is dark.
rules.rationale: The honest output for a broken discovery path is a skip finding, never a silent empty. Same pattern as the bedrock pack's detection tool.

rules.title: Tool manifest not scanned
rules.threat: An unscanned server silently escapes the manifest rules; a clean scan would overstate coverage.
rules.rationale: Every server the scan cannot reach is named in the report, so absence of findings is never mistaken for absence of risk.

rules.title: Drift baseline could not be read
rules.threat: A silently ignored baseline turns drift detection off while the user believes it ran.
rules.rationale: The honest output for a broken baseline is a skip finding, never a silent pass.

rules.title: npm registry lookup skipped
rules.threat: Missing registry data silently disables three rules; a clean report would overstate what was checked.
rules.rationale: Surfacing the skipped lookup keeps the report honest about coverage instead of implying the registry-backed checks passed.
